core_concept: Atomic Structure and the Periodic Table
facts: The atomic number of an element equals the number of protons in its nucleus, which uniquely identifies the element; for example, every carbon atom has exactly 6 protons, and any atom with 6 protons is carbon.
The nucleus contains nearly all of an atom's mass because protons and neutrons are about 1,800 times heavier than electrons, so electrons contribute almost nothing to the mass.

Isotopes of the same element have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons; for instance, carbon-12 has 6 neutrons while carbon-14 has 8 neutrons, yet both are carbon because they have 6 protons.

The periodic table arranges elements in order of increasing atomic number, not atomic mass; this explains why tellurium (atomic number 52) comes before iodine (53) even though tellurium has a higher average atomic mass.

Elements in the same group (vertical column) of the periodic table have the same number of electrons in their outermost energy level, which gives them similar chemical reactivity; for example, all alkali metals (Group 1) react vigorously with water.
An atom is electrically neutral because the number of protons in the nucleus equals the number of electrons surrounding it; a sodium atom has 11 protons and 11 electrons, so its net charge is zero.

The first energy level of an atom can hold up to 2 electrons, and the second energy level can hold up to 8 electrons; this pattern explains why helium (2 electrons) is stable and neon (10 electrons) has a full outer level.
misconceptions: Students often think that the atomic number is the total number of particles in the nucleus (protons plus neutrons), confusing it with the mass number.
Students believe that all atoms of a given element have exactly the same mass, not realizing that isotopes exist with different numbers of neutrons.

Students assume that elements in the same row (period) of the periodic table have similar chemical properties because they are arranged horizontally, but actually properties repeat vertically in groups.
